title: Motives of the Hitchin system
speaker: Junliang Shen
abstract: Topology of the Hitchin system has been studied for decades, and interesting connections were found to orbital integrals, non-abelian Hodge theory, mirror symmetry etc. I will explain that a large part of the symmetries in these geometries above are “motivated” by algebraic cycles; in particular, I will discuss a proof of the motivic decomposition conjecture for the Hitchin system, and a proof of the motivic “\chi”-independence phenomenon. In the proofs, the desired algebraic cycles are constructed by a combination of the the Fourier-Mukai duality for the derived category of coherent sheaves and Springer theory. Based on joint work with Davesh Maulik and Qizheng Yin.

proof_stages.label: Categorical symmetries as motivic correspondences
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the problem of converting Fourier--Mukai-type symmetries of derived categories into algebraic cycles acting on cohomology and motives, and asks for this bridge in the Hitchin system.

proof_stages.label: GL_n Hitchin fibration setup
proof_stages.summary: For coprime rank n and degree d over a genus at least two curve, the speaker defines stable Higgs bundles, the Hitchin map by characteristic polynomials to the base B_n, and recalls its proper Lagrangian fibration geometry with Jacobian-type generic fibers and complicated singular fibers.

proof_stages.label: Perverse decomposition and P=W target
proof_stages.summary: The BBDG decomposition theorem gives perverse cohomology sheaves and a perverse filtration on the cohomology of the Higgs moduli space, while Ngô-type support results and the P=W theorem identify this filtration with the weight filtration on the corresponding character variety.

proof_stages.label: Relative hard Lefschetz symmetry
proof_stages.summary: A relative ample class for the Hitchin map gives relative hard Lefschetz isomorphisms between perverse cohomology sheaves, which under P=W become the curious hard Lefschetz symmetry for the character variety, with discussion of noncanonical versus Hodge-theoretic splittings.

proof_stages.label: Degree-independence of relative cohomology
proof_stages.summary: For two coprime degrees d and d', the relative cohomology complexes over the same Hitchin base are isomorphic, so the singular cohomology of the Higgs moduli spaces is degree-independent even though ordinary vector-bundle moduli are not.

proof_stages.label: Motivic decomposition by Chow projectors
proof_stages.summary: The decomposition theorem for the Hitchin map is lifted to a decomposition of the relative diagonal into idempotent Chow projectors pi_i whose induced action realizes the perverse decomposition and whose concrete form comes from Fourier--Mukai/Poincaré-type correspondences.

proof_stages.label: Motivic Lefschetz and motivic degree-independence
proof_stages.summary: The inverse Lefschetz operator is represented by an algebraic cycle, proving the relevant Lefschetz standard conjecture for the Hitchin map, and the motivic summands of the relative cohomology are isomorphic for different degrees.

proof_stages.label: Construction via compactified Jacobians and Springer reduction
proof_stages.summary: The proof constructs the needed correspondences first on compactified Jacobians of integral locally planar spectral curves using an extended Poincaré Cohen--Macaulay sheaf and its characteristic classes, then uses Springer theory to reduce the full Hitchin system with worse spectral curves to that case.

examples.name: Abelian schemes and Mukai Fourier transform
examples.note: The abelian-scheme Fourier--Mukai/Poincaré-line-bundle story is presented as the model showing how a derived-category symmetry yields algebraic-cycle projectors, motivating the analogous Hitchin-system construction.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker traces the abelian-scheme case through Mukai’s Fourier transform, the multiplication-by-n operator on homology, its eigenspace decomposition, and the motivic projectors from the Poincaré line bundle.

passes.reason: Used as the smooth group-scheme prototype with Fourier--Mukai duality rather than to refute a claim.
Abelian schemes with the Poincaré/Mukai Fourier transform are used as a positive model of the symmetry-to-motive mechanism, with no failure claim.
Abelian schemes are smooth group varieties with strong translation/Fourier–Mukai duality symmetry and are used as a motivating model, not to refute a claim.
An ordinary abelian scheme with Mukai's Fourier transform is used as a positive model for motivic/cohomological decompositions, not as a failure case.
Abelian schemes are highly regular smooth group-scheme fibrations with Fourier–Mukai duality, used as the motivating positive model rather than a refutation.
Abelian schemes have intrinsic group/Fourier-duality symmetries and are used as the standard motivating model, not to refute a claim.
It is used as a positive motivating model, with the abelian group/dual Fourier–Mukai symmetry giving motivic projectors.

used as a positive prototype where the group/dual symmetry via Mukai Fourier transform yields motivic projectors

Used as a positive model for Fourier–Mukai/motivic decompositions, and abelian schemes have homogeneous group-scheme fibers giving strong intrinsic symmetry.

examples.name: Stable vector-bundle moduli in different degrees
examples.note: Ordinary moduli spaces of stable vector bundles of degrees one and two are contrasted with Higgs-moduli spaces to illustrate why degree-independence for the Hitchin system is surprising and depends on correction from the rest of the Hitchin fiber.
examples.engagement_reason: Stable vector-bundle moduli in different degrees are used as a contrast, with facts stated about differing Betti numbers and zero-fiber components but no calculation or construction carried out on them.

passes.reason: Ordinary stable vector-bundle moduli in degrees 1 and 2 are cited to show degree-independence of Betti numbers fails without Higgs fields.
Stable vector-bundle moduli in different degrees are cited to show degree-independence of Betti/cohomology fails for ordinary bundle moduli.
Ordinary stable vector-bundle moduli in different coprime degrees are cited because their Betti numbers can differ, refuting the analogous degree-independence statement.
Stable vector-bundle moduli in different degrees are ordinary moduli spaces, used to show the analogous degree-independence statement fails for vector bundles.
Stable vector-bundle moduli in different degrees are ordinary moduli spaces here used to show degree-independence fails without Higgs fields.
The stable vector-bundle moduli spaces in different degrees are ordinary moduli spaces, cited to show degree-independence fails for vector bundles unlike for Hitchin moduli.
Ordinary stable vector-bundle moduli in different degrees are cited because their Betti numbers differ, showing the analogous degree-independence statement fails.
Stable vector-bundle moduli in two degrees are cited because their Betti numbers differ, showing the analogous degree-independence statement fails.
stable vector-bundle moduli in different degrees are cited to show degree-independence of Betti numbers fails in that setting
Stable vector-bundle moduli in different degrees are invoked to show degree-independence fails for ordinary bundle moduli, unlike the Hitchin case.
Stable vector-bundle moduli in different coprime degrees are ordinary moduli spaces but are cited to show degree-independence of cohomology fails without adding Higgs fields.
The stable vector-bundle moduli in different degrees are invoked to show degree-independence of cohomology fails outside the Higgs setting, while the moduli spaces themselves are ordinary examples.

examples.name: Base-point case of motivic decomposition
examples.note: Mapping a smooth projective variety to a point is mentioned to identify the general motivic decomposition conjecture with Murre's Chow--Künneth conjecture, clarifying the strength of the Hitchin result.
examples.engagement_reason: The base-point case is cited as the special case of the motivic decomposition conjecture giving Murre’s Chow–Künneth conjecture, without working through an example or deriving projectors.

passes.reason: Taking the base to be a point is the collapsed base case reducing the relative conjecture to Murre's Chow--Künneth conjecture.
The base-a-point specialization is the minimal collapsed case of the relative motivic decomposition conjecture, not a refutation.
Taking the base to be a point is the collapsed minimal base case of the motivic-decomposition conjecture, used only as a special case.
The base being a point is the collapsed minimal base case of the motivic decomposition conjecture, cited as the Murre/Chow–Künneth special case.
The base-point case collapses the relative situation to a smooth projective variety over a point, serving as a limiting base case of the conjecture.
The map to a point is the collapsed minimal-base case of the motivic decomposition conjecture, used as a reduction to Murre’s conjecture rather than as a counterexample.
The base reduced to a point is the collapsed/minimal absolute case of the relative motivic decomposition conjecture, used as a specialization rather than a refutation.
The map to a point is the collapsed minimal-base case of the relative motivic decomposition conjecture, used for comparison with Murre's conjecture.
the base-point case collapses the relative motivic decomposition conjecture to the minimal absolute Chow–Künneth/Murre setting
The base equal to a point is the collapsed minimal case of the relative motivic decomposition conjecture, reducing it to Murre/Chow-Künneth decomposition.
The map to a point is the collapsed base case of the motivic decomposition conjecture, reducing it to Murre’s Chow–Künneth setting.
